paragraphs.body: A study in Character: Bird by Bird, Angela, The Breaking Point, and Sibling Rivalry

I’m choosing to focus on character for the purposes of this craft paper. I chose this because it was often the aspect of writing I ignored the most as a writer, as I assumed that my readers knew as much about the characters as I did, which led to a lot of confusion within the proofreading process. Trying to flesh out a character was one of the most entertaining aspects of writing, and my only regret is not having started it sooner.

annotations.context_left: I’m choosing to focus on cha
annotations.context_right: for the purposes of this craft paper. I chose this because it was often the aspect of writing I ign

annotations.comment: I agree — I love character (and dialogue) the most!

paragraphs.body: The title mentions the pieces I will focus on as requirements, but I’d like to start by taking a look at The Lesson. This story did a wonderful job of setting up the characters, and not just by way of having the narrator list off her friends’ quirks. Rather, the narrator just narrates the plot, pointing out details that matter to her, details that only come about as a matter of having spent time with these people for so long. For instance, in the scene where the main character and her friends are summoned by Ms. Moore, the first instinct is to point out how much she hates this, as stated, “And the starch in my pinafore scratching the shit outta me and I’m really hating this nappy-head bitch and her goddamn college degree.” I think this is important in terms of setting up the entire conflict; it is abundantly clear that the narrator dislikes Ms. Moore, because this level of ranting is only possible if you are fundamentally opposed to someone. This is something that had to have been planned in advance, a result of the influence that characters are having on each other. This moves the plot along naturally via emotional influence, rather than forcing the issue through clunky dialogue or awkward narration. I noticed this as I was doing another readthrough of Angela, where I had Sally work through her emotions in a different way; repressing them until the time came to let them out. Although not done as fluidly, I learned how much emotions could influence a story and am trying to implement that in The Breaking Point, where the two siblings are dealing with the fallout of an argument with their parents. My plan for the second draft has been to focus only on Milo, and to highlight his emotional response to drastic events in his life.

The focus on character is present in Sibling Rivalry, although it is not the main focus. I just chose this piece because I liked the way the character’s personalities were revealed through dialogue and narration, centering their response to this strange world as the focus of the plot. As said in Bird by Bird, “You may only know your characters’ externals instead of their essences. Don’t worry about it.” Sibling Rivalry’s scene where the neighbors are discussing the classes of artificial humans does a good job of portraying this concept. Despite the fact that roughly four or five characters are present in the scene with no introduction, I got the sense that they were all very distinct, and their responses to certain stimuli started to make sense. This is only further amplified for the main character, and thus, this is a style of writing I’d like to shape mine to match (given my own plethora of side characters). I tried to touch on this briefly in Angela, when Ayomi was introduced; although the reader was not formally introduced to her, I tried to pay special attention to the impression she would leave on the reader. By impressing upon them the fact that this was a character who cared for Sally, and allowed her a haven to joke around with, I think although Ayomi is not the solution to Sally’s problems, she is a welcome distraction.

I did not stop to think about this before I took this class, but a line in Bird by Bird that stood out to me was, “Another way to familiarize yourself with your characters is to base them partly on someone you know, a model from real life or a composite--”. I hadn’t stopped to consider the implications of this; I tried to stay away from this advice, due to the fear of going too far in the direction of modeling. But having read this line in Bird by Bird, I noticed that I had already been doing so. Sally has my business career and desire to be the best at what she does (although, only she has the execution down), and Ayomi has my desire to encourage and stick by my friends through their tough times. While these do not comprise the entirety of their characters, basing characters out of slices of me has proven to be much easier than choosing to focus on a character with entirely foreign traits. I found that our classmates did the same; just today (the 17th), Dante’s story focusing on dogs was based on the fact that he owned several dogs of his own, and that drove his own story. This made for a more authentic experience, as if I were to write about dogs, I wouldn’t have the same level of success as he had by putting his feelings into the work.

annotations.comment: Yes, in my experience almost all of us do this naturally, if perhaps subconsciously!
Yes, for sure! That’s not to say we can’t study or research to build a character but it does seem to me to be more believable when based on someone real (including ourselves).

paragraphs.body: Finally, I want to discuss the use of the narrator. In Bird by Bird (in the Character section), Anne Lamott recalls a discussion she had with Ethan Canin, who stated, “Nothing is as important as a likable narrator. Nothing holds a story together better.” I firmly agree with this statement. Not only must the narrator be likeable, they must be the correct person to narrate events. If we considered Cell One, there was a conscious choice to have events narrated by the sister. In Sibling Rivalry, there is no direct narrator, but a focus around the Burkhart’s. There is enough information revealed in just these different viewpoints that allows for the reader to glean a sense of the situation the characters find themselves in. The discussion around Cell One specifically made me focus on this more than normal, as choosing a character who was in the right place was a difficult task. I found this to be the case in my first draft of The Breaking Point. I had two viewpoints going into it, switching from Milo into Lightning. However, I believe it will be better, considering the circumstances, for Milo to be the primary focus, and to be a spectator of the tragedies happening around him (similar to Cell One, even). By implementing this, I hope to grant the reader a better understanding of how such issues can affect even those who aren’t directly involved. Because Milo is only 10 at the start of the story, I hope to paint it in a slightly less detailed light before transitioning into him knowing the full truth and coming to his own conclusions. The unreliable narrator is always a fun method.

annotations.comment: I would actually say that being correct is more important than likable!

paragraphs.body: I hope that, going forward, I will remember these lessons and keep them in mind going forward, including aspects of Bird by Bird that I didn’t focus on in this paper.

annotations.comment: I hope so too! Truly a pleasure to have you in class, Joshua. You’re a good writer and I hope you will keep at it!
